summary:
Beidha is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic B (PPNB) site located in southern Jordan near the spectacular Nabataean city of Petra, excavated by Diana Kirkbride in the 1960s and later re-studied in subsequent campaigns. Occupied from approximately 7200 to 6500 BCE, Beidha provides important evidence for the development of early farming communities in the southern Levant during a period when the agricultural transition was well underway but pottery had not yet been adopted.

The site went through several architectural phases, with earlier round or subcircular structures giving way to more rectilinear, multi-room buildings in later phases — a transition that mirrors the broader Neolithic architectural evolution seen across the Levant during this period. The inhabitants of Beidha cultivated emmer wheat and lentils and herded goats, while continuing to hunt wild animals and gather wild plant foods. Evidence of craft specialization, including specialized production of bone tools and beads, suggests an emerging division of labor.

Beidha is also notable for evidence of communal facilities and possible public buildings, suggesting a degree of organized community life beyond the household level. Its location in the arid southern highlands of Jordan demonstrates the wide geographic range across which early Neolithic farming communities established themselves during this era, finding productive niches even in relatively dry environments by exploiting seasonal resources and maintaining flexibility in their subsistence strategies.
